self_report.reflection: Substituting t = u^2 converts the integral to 2 e^{-2a} ∫_0^∞ e^{-a(u-1/u)^2} du. The symmetry u → 1/u together with dv = (1+1/u^2)du, v = u - 1/u, turns the remaining integral into (1/2)√(π/a) via the given Gaussian. Multiplying gives √(π/a) e^{-2a}, exactly the asserted closed form.
self_report.notes: Classical Putnam 1985 B5; standard t=u^2 plus Glasser u↔1/u symmetry reproduces the stated answer.
